hallucinations.description: The paper describes the Uncertainty Estimator baseline as using equal weights (1/3 each) to combine three disagreement metrics, but the actual code in disagreement_analysis.py uses unequal weights: semantic_dispersion=0.4, cluster_diversity=0.3, length_variance=0.3. The paper's formula states confidence = 1 - (D_semantic + D_cluster + D_length) / 3, but the code computes composite_uncertainty with weights 0.4, 0.3, 0.3.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 3.5: "Uncertainty Estimator: Composite metric with equal weights: \\hat{p}_{ue} = 1 - \\frac{D_{semantic} + D_{cluster} + D_{length}}{3}" vs. Code in disagreement_analysis.py: "weights = {'semantic_dispersion': 0.4, 'cluster_diversity': 0.3, 'length_variance': 0.3}"

hallucinations.description: The paper claims the calibration network has 25,249 parameters (Section 6.1.1), but the actual architecture described (387→128→64→32→1) would have 387*128+128 + 128*64+64 + 64*32+32 + 32*1+1 = 49,536 + 8,256 + 2,080 + 33 = 59,905 parameters (or similar count depending on dropout layers which have no parameters). The stated 25,249 is incorrect.
hallucinations.evidence: Section 6.1.1: "With only 140 training samples, the 4-layer neural network (25,249 parameters) likely overfit to spurious patterns"

hallucinations.description: Reference [12] 'Wang, X., et al. (2024). Ensemble Methods for Large Language Models' appears to be a fabricated citation. No such paper with this exact title and author can be verified. The citation is vague with 'et al.' and lacks specific venue information.
hallucinations.evidence: [12] Wang, X., et al. (2024). Ensemble Methods for Large Language Models.

hallucinations.description: References [9] and [10] appear to cite the same paper (Xu, Z., et al., 2025, 'Self-ensemble: Mitigating Confidence Mis-calibration for Large Language Models') but are listed as separate references and used to describe different methods. Reference [9] is described as 'Token-Level Probability Methods' while [10] is described as 'Self-Verbalized Confidence' and 'Self-Ensemble Methods', creating confusion about what each actually refers to.
hallucinations.evidence: [9] Xu, Z., Wang, G., Zheng, G., Chuang, Y.-N., Szalay, A., Hu, X., & Braverman, V. (2025). Self-ensemble: Mitigating Confidence Mis-calibration for Large Language Models. [10] Xu, Z., et al. (2025). Self-ensemble: Mitigating Confidence Mis-calibration for Large Language Models.

hallucinations.description: The paper describes the Sharpness metric as 'Sharpness = (1/n) * sum(max(p_i, 1-p_i))' in Section 4.3.3, but the code in evaluation.py computes sharpness simply as the mean of confidences: 'np.mean(confidences)'. These are different calculations - the paper's formula would always produce values >= 0.5, while the code's implementation can produce any value in [0,1].
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 4.3.3: "\\text{Sharpness} = \\frac{1}{n}\\sum_{i=1}^{n} \\max(\\hat{p}_i, 1-\\hat{p}_i)" vs. Code evaluation.py: "return float(np.mean(confidences))". The majority_voting baseline has sharpness 0.278 in results, which would be impossible under the paper's formula (minimum would be 0.5).

overall_assessment: The paper contains several hallucinations of varying severity. The most significant are methodological discrepancies between what the paper describes and what the code actually implements (unequal vs. equal weights for the Uncertainty Estimator, incorrect sharpness formula, wrong parameter count). There is also a likely fabricated citation [12] and a duplicate/confused citation pair [9]/[10]. The experimental results themselves appear genuine as they match the metrics.json output, but the paper misrepresents aspects of the methodology used to produce them.
